digest: Startups don't take off on their own — founders make them take off, like hand-cranking a car engine before it can run by itself
The most common unscalable move is recruiting users manually and one-by-one, instead of waiting for them to come to you (e.g. Stripe's 'Collison installation', Airbnb going door-to-door)
Founders resist this out of shyness/laziness and because early absolute numbers look tiny — but they underestimate compound weekly growth (10%/week turns 100 users into millions in two years)
Almost all startups are extremely fragile at first and get misjudged by the standards of established companies; the right question is 'how big could this get if the founders did the right things?'
Take extraordinary, unscalable measures to delight early users (hand-written notes, personal attention) — a level of service big companies literally can't provide
Aim for an 'insanely great' experience, not necessarily an insanely great product: attentiveness can make up for an early, buggy, incomplete product
Direct engagement with earliest users is also the best feedback loop for making the product good
Start in a deliberately narrow market to reach critical mass (Facebook starting at Harvard) — like containing a fire to get it hot before adding logs
Hardware startups can 'pull a Meraki' by assembling units themselves; B2B startups can act as consultants building for a single user, or do tasks manually before automating
Tactics that usually fail: the Big Launch and partnerships with big companies — any strategy that omits real effort is suspect
Think of a startup idea as a vector: what you'll build plus the unscalable things you'll do to start — and ideally those unscalable habits permanently shape the company's DNA
